segments.s352-g97: سخن گفتن is a verbal-noun + infinitive construction meaning 'to speak' or 'speaking' (verbal noun functioning as subject). اکنون is an adverb 'now/at present' modifying the clause. نیاید به کار is an impersonal idiom: نیاید (3rd-person negative form of آمدن used idiomatically) + به کار ('to/for use') = 'is not of use' or 'is not fitting'. Word order is typical older Persian (verbal idea after verbal noun).
segments.s352-g98: گه introduces a circumstance/time ('on the occasion of' or 'when'); it governs the following nouns. جنگ و آویزش are two coordinated nouns linked by و ('and'), and آویزش کارزار is a compact poetic noun-group meaning the 'grapple/engagement of the battle' (آویزش in an abstract/nominal sense linked to کارزار). The phrase functions adverbially to specify the situation in which speech is useless.
segments.s352-g99: This has two coordinated past actions: برگشت (past 'returned') و آمد (past 'came'), a common narrative sequence in Persian. چو باد is a simile 'like the wind' modifying آمد and placed after the verbs for poetic effect; word order places the manner phrase after the motion verbs.
segments.s352-g100: همی کرد is an archaic/periphrastic tense marker (همی + past verb) giving a sense of immediate or repeated action — here 'did/was doing' and often idiomatically 'swiftly did'. پاسخ is the direct object ('an answer/reply') and follows the verb (typical older Persian SOV ordering). به الیاس یاد is a short prepositional/adverbial phrase meaning 'in (the) memory of Elias' or 'recalling Elias'; the order is slightly inverted for poetic compactness (more expected modern order would be به یاد الیاس).
segments.s352-g65: ز سوی خزر is a prepositional phrase meaning 'from the direction/side of the Caspian' (ز + سوی + place). نای رویین is a noun (نای) followed by its adjective (رویین), 'an iron/brass pipe/trumpet' — classical Persian places the adjective after the noun. بخاست is a literary past intransitive verb ('rose up', 'sounded') that completes the clause.
segments.s352-g66: همی is a poetic particle marking ongoing or immediate action; گرد here names the dust/whirl (a noun), and بر شد is the compound meaning 'rose up' (بر + شد, with شد as past tense 'became/occurred'). سوی چرخ راست is a directional phrase ('toward the sky to the right'), with سوی introducing the direction, چرخ = 'sky', and راست indicating 'to the right'.
segments.s352-g67: سرافراز قیصر is a nominal phrase with an epithet (سرافراز 'triumphant') before the head noun قیصر 'Caesar/emperor'. به گشتاسپ گفت is 'said to Gushtasp' — به marks the indirect object and گفت is the past-tense verb 'said'.
segments.s352-g68: که introduces reported speech or the content of what was said ('that / saying'). اکنون is the temporal adverb 'now'. جدا کن is an imperative construction: جدا (apart) + کن (imperative 'do'), so 'separate' or 'bring forth'. سپاه is the object 'army'; از نهفت literally 'from concealment' — از + نهفت (hidden/hidden place) gives the source 'out of hiding.'
segments.s352-g69: بگفت این is a compact literary past phrase 'he said this' (ب- prefix + گفت). و connects clauses. لشکر به بیرون کشید is a transitive clause where به بیرون ('to the outside/out') is the directional complement and کشید (past of کشیدن) means 'drew/led/moved' — 'he drew the host out.'
segments.s352-g70: This continues with a coordinated object: گوان و یلان را — two coordinated nouns marked by را as the definite object marker. به هامون کشید repeats the verb کشید with the directional phrase 'to the plain' (هامون) — 'he led X to the plain.'
segments.s352-g71: همی گشت is a poetic past clause where همی marks continuous or immediate action and گشت is a past of گشتن 'he went/turned/marched'. با گرزه is an instrumental phrase 'with a mace'. گاوسار follows as a proper name in apposition (the warrior's name).
segments.s352-g72: چو is the simile particle 'like/as'. سرو بلند is noun + postpositive adjective 'a tall cypress' (classical order: noun then adjective). از بر کوهسار is a poetic locative phrase 'from/atop the mountain-side' (از + بر + place) that completes the simile.
segments.s352-g49: چو introduces a temporal clause meaning 'when/while/as'; من باره اندر جهانم is a poetic way of saying 'I, in my time/in my life in the world' (باره = 'occasion, one's turn; life/period') and به خاک adds the locative 'on earth'. Word order is topical/poetic (چو + subject phrase + locative).
segments.s352-g50: ندارم is the present tense negative of داشتن 'I do not have / I have no'; ز marks the source/against ('from' / 'with respect to'); مرکب مرز خزر = 'the Khazar frontier'; هیچ باک = 'no fear/concern'. The clause states lack of fear in simple present.
segments.s352-g51: ولیکن is a formal conjunction 'but / however'; نباید is impersonal modal 'one should not / it ought not'; که introduces the subordinate clause; روز نبرد is a genitival/compound noun 'the day of battle' (noun + noun in classical word order).
segments.s352-g52: ز introduces the object/source of remembering ('from / about'); میرین and اهرن are proper names (quoted here); بود یاد کرد is a past tense narrative phrasing 'it was remembered / one remembered' with بود + کرد forming a simple past narrative—together meaning 'to recall / to mention'.
segments.s352-g53: که introduces a subordinate clause ('that/for'); ایشان is the subject 'they'; به رزم اندر = 'into/in battle' (به + noun gives 'into/toward'); از دشمنی = 'out of/because of enmity' (از marking motive/source). The sequence places circumstantial phrases (place and cause) before the verb in classical order.
segments.s352-g54: برآرند is a 3rd-person plural present/subjunctive form meaning 'they may bring forth / they produce' (optative/arranging result after که); کژی و آهرمنی are the objects 'crookedness / wrongness and demonic (Ahrimanic) evil'. The verb is used in a potentialive sense following که/نباید context.
segments.s352-g55: چو introduces a conditional/temporal clause 'if/when'; لشکر بیاید uses بیاید (present-subjunctive or optative) meaning 'an army comes/should come'; ز مرز خزر = 'from the Khazar border' with ز marking origin. The clause is a protasis (condition/time) preceding the imperative.
segments.s352-g56: نگهبان من = 'my guard/guardian' (noun phrase); باش is the 2nd-person singular imperative 'be!'; با یک پسر 'with one son' expresses accompaniment/instrument. The sequence is imperative: 'Be my guard, together with one son.'
segments.s352-g25: سخن (news/word) is the theme placed before the verb; چون here functions as a temporal conjunction 'when' (an archaic/poetic use of چون). به marks the indirect object ('to/at') and میرین و اهرن are the recipients; رسید is a past-tense, 3rd-person singular verb 'reached/arrived'. The clause thus reads literally 'When the news reached Mīrīn and Ahrān.'
segments.s352-g26: ز is the poetic form of the preposition 'from/about' introducing the source or content of the news; الیاس names the person concerned. و آن دام کو گسترید is a noun phrase 'and that trap which he spread/la id' — کو functions here in a relative/archaic sense ('which/that') and گسترید is the past tense 'spread/la id'. The whole segment qualifies what the news was about.
segments.s352-g27: فرستاد is a past-tense, 3rd-person singular verb 'sent' with میرین as its subject; the prepositional phrase به قیصر پیام places the object after the verb common in Persian ('a message to Caesar'). The clause is a simple S + V + (prep + object) construction.
segments.s352-g28: که introduces reported content ('that...'). این اژدها نیست negates the predicate: 'this dragon is not...' The following کاید به دام is an archaic/construction meaning 'to be taken/caught in a trap' — نیست negates that capability. به دام is the locative/goal phrase 'into a trap.'
segments.s352-g29: نه negates the following identification: گرگست literally 'is a wolf' (گرگ + است fused here); the clause denies that identity ('is not a wolf'). کز introduces a subordinate clause 'from/for which/that' and چاره بیجان شود uses چاره ('remedy/stratagem') with بیجان 'lifeless' and شود (become) to indicate the result — 'would become lifeless for lack of a remedy.'
segments.s352-g30: ز here means 'from/by' and introduces the cause: آلودن is the infinitival/gerundial form 'being tainted/defiled,' زهر 'poison' names the agent, and پیچان is an adjective 'treacherous/deceptive' modifying the poison. شود ('become') completes the result construction — 'would become (dead) from being tainted by treacherous poison.'
segments.s352-g31: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; الیاس is the subject. در جنگ is a locative phrase 'in battle' modifying the verb. خشم آورد is an idiomatic verbal phrase: آورد (past) with خشم ('anger') means 'became angry' or 'brought anger' — here it indicates Elyās's anger arising in battle.
segments.s352-g32: جهانجوی is the affected person ('the world-seeker' or 'ambitious man'), and را is the definite-object/topic marker highlighting him. خون به چشم آورد is a set phrase: خون ('blood') + به چشم ('to the eye') + آورد ('brought') — 'caused blood to come to his eyes' i.e. 'made his eyes fill/bleed.' آورد is again the past verb causing the effect.
segments.s352-g57: به introduces an instrumental/causal phrase ('by' or 'by means of'). نیروی is a genitival/construct noun 'power/force' and here is qualified by پیروزگر (an attributive adjective/epithet 'victorious'). یک خدای completes the genitive relationship: 'the (victorious) power of one god.' The word order is poetic (noun + epithet + possessor) rather than modern colloquial.
segments.s352-g58: چو here functions as a temporal/conditional conjunction 'when/if.' من is the subject, followed by the prepositional phrase با سپاه 'with an army.' اندر آیم uses the poetic/archaic verb form آیم (1st person singular present/subjunctive of آمدن) meaning 'I come/forth'; اندر is an older preposition 'in/within' used here in combination with آیم. ز جای is a poetic 'from place' (ز = 'from') indicating origin ('from my place').
segments.s352-g59: نه ... نه is the correlative negative 'neither ... nor.' الیاس is a proper name; مانم is the archaic/present form of ماندن used here predicatively as 'I am / I remain (not).' The second half is elliptical: 'نه با او سپاه' = 'nor [do I have] an army with him' where the verb is omitted but understood from the first clause.
segments.s352-g60: نه continues the negation. چندن here is a poetic quantifier meaning 'to that extent / so much' (i.e. 'not so great'); بزرگی is 'greatness' or 'great rank.' The coordinated nouns تخت و کلاه list items of worldly power (throne and helmet/helm). Word order and the negative particle produce the sense 'nor such greatness, nor throne and helm.'
segments.s352-g61: کمربند گیرمش is literal: کمربند 'belt' + گیرم (here with enclitic object -ش) 'I seize him/it' = 'I seize his belt.' وز is a contracted poetic sequence (originally و + ز) functioning as 'and from,' so وز پشت زین reads 'and from behind the saddle.' This fusion is a common archaic/poetic compression.
segments.s352-g62: به ابر اندر آرم uses به 'to' + ابر 'cloud' and اندر آرم where آرم is an archaic 1st-person verb form meaning 'I put/bring/place' or 'I raise' (here 'I lift into'), literally 'I set into the cloud.' زنم بر زمین is a coordinated verb phrase: زنم (from زدن) 'I strike/throw' + بر زمین 'upon/to the earth' = 'I strike him down to the ground.' The sequence shows two successive results of the action.
segments.s352-g63: دگر روز 'another/next day' provides the temporal frame. چون here functions as 'as/when' introducing the temporal clause. بردمید is a poetic/archaic perfective/imperfective verb meaning 'rose up / came forth' (here of the sun), and آفتاب is the subject 'the sun.' The whole means 'on the next day, when the sun rose.'
segments.s352-g64: چو introduces a simile/scene ('as' or 'when') and is followed by the simile's noun phrase زرین سپر 'golden shield.' می‌نمود is the imperfective/epistolary form of نمودن 'to show/appear' meaning 'it appeared/was seen.' اندر آب 'in/on the water' gives the location. Word order places the simile before the verb, a common poetic pattern.
segments.s352-g9: اگر (if) introduces a conditional clause. The segment then lists items joined by و (and): two proper names (ساو و باژست) followed by گنج گران, where گنج (treasure) is qualified by the adjective گران (precious/expensive). In classical Persian poetry the copula or verb is often omitted and understood from context, so this whole noun phrase is the protasis (the ‘if’ part) that will be completed by what follows.
segments.s352-g10: This continues the conditional listing: گروگان (hostages) qualified by ازان مرز (from that frontier) — ازان is a fused prepositional phrase meaning 'from that/there'. چندی (several/some) modifies سران (heads/leaders). Word order is nominal (Noun + modifier + prepositional phrase) and again no copula; the clause as a whole is a verbless condition whose verb is implied or will be supplied by the following lines.
segments.s352-g11: وگرنه (otherwise) introduces the alternative. فرخ‌زاد is the proper name (subject). چون پیل مست is a simile: چون (like/as) + پیل (elephant) + مست (drunk/intoxicated) — adjectives can follow nouns in classical verse, so 'like a drunken elephant' modifies the subject.
segments.s352-g12: بیاید کند is a two-part verbal expression: بیاید (may/shall come) + کند (do/make) — in poetic usage this sequence expresses that he will come and effect an action. کشورت را is the definite object (your country/realm) marked by را. چو دست is a simile 'like a hand' where دست = power/control; together the clause means 'he will come and take your realm as (if it were) in his hand.'
segments.s352-g13: چو (when) introduces a temporal clause. الیاس is the subject. بر خواند is a compound/prefixed verb (بر‑ + خواند) here meaning 'read (aloud/upon)' in the past tense. آن نامه را is the definite object with the object marker را — standard S V O order.
segments.s352-g14: به زهر آب is a prepositional phrase literally 'with/into poison-water' (i.e., poisonous water). در زد is a verbal phrase meaning 'struck into' or 'dipped into' (در = in/into + زد = struck/put). سر خامه را (the tip/head of the quill) is the definite object marked by را. Together: 'he dipped the quill's tip into poisonous water.'
segments.s352-g15: چنین داد پاسخ is 'he gave such an answer' (چنین = thus/so, داد = gave, پاسخ = answer). که introduces the content clause that follows. چندین هنر is a quantified noun phrase ('several/many arts/skills'); in context it begins the reported remark that will be negated in the next segment.
segments.s352-g16: نبودی is the negative past copula/verb ('you were not' / 'there was not'); in narrative/reporting it addresses the addressee and negates possession or presence of the preceding quality. به روم اندرون سربسر is a locative/intensive phrase: به روم (in/to Rome), اندرون (within/inside), سربسر (completely/throughout). The whole clause: 'you were not (possessed of) many skills in Rome at all.'
segments.s352-g81: کزین is an archaic/prepositional compound meaning 'from this' (literary). لشکر is the noun 'army' and اکنون 'now' places the time. سوارش is سوار ('rider/horseman') plus the enclitic -ش = 'its'. تویی is the 2nd-person copula 'thou art' placed after the predicate (typical Persian inverted order in poetry). The whole line says ‘of this army, now you are its rider.’
segments.s352-g82: The pattern repeats: بهارش is بهار + -ش = 'its spring/season/ornament' (metaphorical); تویی again 'you are'. نامدارش is the adjective 'renowned/illustrious' with the possessive -ش = 'its famed one', followed by the copula تویی. Persian often places the predicate noun + enclitic before the copula in poetry.
segments.s352-g83: به یکسو گرای is a phrase meaning 'inclined/leaning to one side' (به + یکسو + گرای), where گرای acts as an adjectival/participial element '‑leaning/‑oriented'. از میان دو صف is a prepositional phrase 'out from between the two ranks/lines' (از = from, میان = between, صف = battle line). Together they describe someone having turned aside from the formation.
segments.s352-g84: چه داری is an interrogative 'what do you have' (چه = what; داری = you have). چنین modifies the following clause 'thus/so'. بر لب آورده کف is a verbal cluster where آورده (past participle 'brought/put') with the present auxiliary/possessive داری yields 'you have brought/put' and کف 'the palm' is the thing so brought 'on the lip' (بر لب). The order is idiomatic: 'what have you that you have put your palm to your lips like this?'.
segments.s352-g85: که introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause 'for/that'. الیاس is the proper name Elias. شیر است is the predication 'is a lion' (شیر noun + copula است). روز نبرد is a temporal genitive 'the day of battle' (روز = day, نبرد = battle) modifying the predication.
segments.s352-g86: پذیره is a noun here 'reception/shelter/acceptance' and درآید is the 3rd-person present/subjunctive of 'enter/come in' (narrative: 'he enters/is received'). سبک‌تر is the comparative 'more light(‑weight)', ز = 'than/from' (poetic form), and گرد (here) is used as 'dust'. The clause gives a simile: he is received/enters more lightly than dust.
segments.s352-g87: اگر is the conditional 'if'. هدیه خواهی = 'you want a gift' (هدیه = gift; خواهی = you want). ورا = 'for him/to him' (indirect object or beneficiary). گنج هست is an existential clause 'there is treasure' (گنج = treasure; هست = there is). Word order places the desired object and indirect object before the existential predicate, as is common in Persian.
segments.s352-g88: مسای is a literary form of 'مساء' meaning 'in the evening' or 'later'. از پی literally 'in pursuit of / after' (here marking the attempt to get something). چیز = 'thing/anything'. با رنج دست is an instrumental phrase 'with the toil/hardship of the hand' (با = with, رنج = hardship/toil, دست = hand) — the verb is omitted in the poetry, so the meaning is 'to get anything later requires manual toil.'
segments.s352-g17: This conditional clause begins with the protasis particle 'اگر' (if). 'من' is the 1st-person subject. 'نخواهم' is the negative present/future of 'خواستن' here meaning 'I do not wish' (negating the desire). 'همی' is the older continuous/emphatic particle attached to the verb phrase (classical Persian marker, here adding emphasis rather than literal time). 'باژ روم' names the destination 'Rome' (classical 'روم') with an older locational expression; the whole phrase functions as the verb complement 'to go to Rome'.
segments.s352-g18: 'شما شاد باشید' is a jussive/optative construction: 'may you be happy' (the verb 'باشید' here expresses a wish/imperative for the 2nd-person plural). 'زان' (= از آن) points back to 'that' (from that). 'مرز و بوم' is a binary noun phrase 'frontier and native soil / realm' — 'زان مرز و بوم' means 'with/at that realm and land'.
segments.s352-g19: 'چنین' ('thus/so') adverbially modifies the verbal phrase that follows. 'دل گرفتید' is a 2nd-person plural past construction from 'دل گرفتن' meaning 'your heart was seized' → 'you were distressed/struck in heart'. 'از یک سوار' gives the cause/agent of that distress: 'because of / by a single rider.' Word order is adverb – predicate – adjunct.
segments.s352-g20: The relative/conjunction 'که' introduces a clause explaining or reporting the occurrence. 'نزد شما' means 'near you / in your presence'; classical Persian often places the locative before the verb. 'یافت او' here uses 'یافت' in the sense 'was found / came to be' (passive/experiential sense), with the pronoun 'او' after the verb for emphasis. 'زینهار' is an interjection 'beware / take heed' placed at the end as a warning.
segments.s352-g21: 'چنان دان' is an imperative/edict: 'consider (it) so' or 'know it thus' — 'دان' here is the verb 'to regard/consider'. 'که' introduces the content clause that follows. 'او دام آهرمنست' is a nominal clause: 'he is a trap (دام) of Ahriman' — note the archaic predicate 'آهرمنست' (Ahriman + -ست = 'is Ahriman's/related to Ahriman' or 'is of Ahriman').
segments.s352-g22: 'و‌گر' (= و اگر) opens a concessive/alternative 'and if / or if'. 'کوه آهن' is a noun phrase 'a mountain of iron' (noun + material). 'همان' is a demonstrative/emphatic 'even so / nevertheless', and 'یکتنست' is an older fused form meaning 'is one person/one man' (yektan-est). The clause contrasts great appearance/strength with the fact that he remains only one individual.
segments.s352-g23: This is an imperative prohibition addressed to 'تو' (singular 'you'): 'تو ... رنجه مکن' = 'do not cause him suffering / do not afflict him'. 'او را' marks the person affected (object). 'بدین جنگ' ('ب' + 'دین' in the segmentation) functions as a locative/adverbial 'in this battle / in this fight' (classical 'به + این' → 'بدین'). Word order is subject – object – adverbial – negative imperative verb.
segments.s352-g24: 'که' explains the reason: 'for / because'. 'من' is the subject 'I'. The clause says the speaker will not lengthen his speech: 'درازی نمانم سخن' = 'my speech will not remain long' → idiomatically 'I will not be long in speaking' (i.e. 'I won't prolong my talk'). 'نمانم' is first-person negative of 'ماندن' (to remain); 'سخن' is the thing that would be long (speech).
segments.s352-g1: This is a predicative clause with a locative/beneficiary phrase first: به (to/for) + قیصر (the Caesar) and then the subject خزر with the past copula بود and the comparative adjective نزدیکتر. Word order is poetic (preposing the indirect locative); بود + نزدیکتر = “was nearer.”
segments.s352-g2: وزیشان here functions as an archaic/poetic causal phrase (‘because of them’ / ‘by their burden’), followed by بدش (بد + enclitic ش = ‘it was bad for him’ or ‘bad to him’) and the time/phrase روز تاریکتر (‘a darker day’). The clause says the consequence: on account of them his day became worse/darker.
segments.s352-g3: به مرز خزر is a locative/directional phrase (to/on the Khazar border) placed before the nominal predicate. مهتر الیاس بود = ‘the chief was Elias’; بود is the past copula, and the name follows مهتر as predicate nominative in poetic word order.
segments.s352-g4: که is a relative pronoun meaning ‘who/which’; پور introduces a patronymic (‘son of’), followed by the father’s name جهاندار مهراس as a compound name; بود is again the past copula. The whole clause identifies Elias’ parentage.
segments.s352-g5: به الیاس is the dative (‘to Elias’), followed by the subject قیصر (the Caesar) in front of the object یکی نامه (a certain letter). کرد is the past verb ‘made/did’ used here as ‘sent/wrote’ (typical Persian use: نامه کردن = to write/send a letter).
segments.s352-g6: تو گفتی is the 2nd-person past ‘you said’ (introducing reported speech); که is the complementizer; خون بر سر خامه کرد is a verb phrase where خون (blood) is placed first, بر سر خامه = ‘upon the tip/top of the pen’, and کرد finishes the reported action (‘made/caused’ → ‘stained/dipped’). Word order (object/PP before verb) is typical in Persian reporting.
segments.s352-g7: که begins a causal/explanatory clause (‘for’ / ‘that’). چندین is a quantifier ‘many/several’; به افسوس خوردی is an idiomatic expression meaning ‘suffered grief/regret’ where خوردی (here from خوردن in the sense ‘befall/happen’) denotes experiencing or enduring something; خزر (the Khazars) appears after the verb in poetic order.
segments.s352-g8: کنون = ‘now’; روز آسایش is a temporal noun phrase ‘a day of ease/comfort’; آمد بسر is an idiomatic compound (‘came upon / has arrived for them’) with آمد as the past verb and بسر marking the coming-to-pass of that time.
segments.s352-g33: نگه کن کنون is an imperative clause: نگه کن is the command 'look!/behold!' (نگه + the imperative کن). کنون is a time adverb 'now' placed after the imperative for emphasis. کاین introduces the following noun phrase as a demonstrative ('this/that'), so the whole segment functions as an attention‑getter: 'Look now at this lofty man.'
segments.s352-g34: ازو چند پیچد is an exclamatory/observational clause describing motion. ازو is a prepositional pronoun 'from him/of him' marking relation to the turning, چند 'how' (exclamative 'how/what a degree'), and پیچد is a present/narrative-form verb 'turns/turns around'. به دشت نبرد is the motion/goal phrase 'toward the plain of battle' (به + place + نبرد ‘battle’ as the domain). Word order is flexible and poetic: the verb follows the question/adverbial word and governs the final locative phrase.
segments.s352-g35: غمی گشت is a predicative construction: غمی 'a sorrow' (predicative noun) + گشت 'became/turned' gives 'became sorrowful.' قیصر is placed after the verb (classical Persian permits verb–subject order). ز گفتارشان uses the preposition ز 'from/because of' to mark cause: 'because of their speech/words.'
segments.s352-g36: چو here is a temporal conjunction 'when.' بشنید is simple past 'he heard.' زان گونه بازارشان is a genitive phrase 'of that kind (زان گونه) their bazaar/marketplace' (گونه 'manner/type' modified by بازارشان). The clause as a whole means 'when he heard of that sort/manner of their market.'
segments.s352-g37: فرخ‌زاد را گفت is object‑first word order common in classical Persian: the name + را (definite/object marker) followed by گفت 'he said (to)'. The following predicate پر مایه‌ای is the content of the utterance: پر 'full (of)' + مایه‌ای 'a source/resource; wealthy one' = 'a wealthy/wealth‑laden one.' Here this phrase functions as what was said to Farrokhzad.
segments.s352-g38: همی before a verbal or nominal phrase is a classical particle indicating continuation or immediacy (here giving an ongoing/intentional sense). روم را can be read either as the place name 'Rome' with را (object/definite marker) or, in older diction, as the verbal form 'I go' (context makes the reading ambiguous in isolation); همچو پیرایه‌ای is a simile 'like an ornament' (همچو + پیرایه + indefinite suffix -ای). Poetic ellipsis/flexible order allows the line to mean roughly 'I will go to Rome like an ornament' or 'I will make Rome like an ornament.'
segments.s352-g39: چنان دان is an imperative/formula 'consider thus' (چنان 'so/thus' + دان 'consider/know' in the sense 'take it so'). که introduces the clause that follows: 'that Elias is Shirā-oozhān' (subject الیاس + epithet شیراوژن + copula است). The copula است finishes the identifying clause.
segments.s352-g40: چو here is temporal 'when.' اسپ افگند is a compact past action 'the steed threw (cast off/overthrew)' (اسپ subject, افگند past verb). پیل رویین‌تن است is a nominal/canonical clause 'he/it is an iron‑bodied elephant' (پیل 'elephant' + رویین‌تن 'iron‑bodied/armored' + copula است). The passage uses vivid metaphor and flexible subject reference: the moment the steed cast him, he is likened to an iron‑bodied elephant.
segments.s352-g89: ز (z) is the poetic preposition 'from' and precedes the noun گیتی (gīti, 'world'), so ز گیتی means 'from the world.' گزین کن is a two-part imperative: گزین (choose/select) plus the light verb کن (do) forming the command 'choose!' یکی بهره‌ای is the object phrase 'one share/portion,' where یکی (one/a certain) quantifies بهره (share) and the -ای on بهره is an indefinite/poetic ending.
segments.s352-g90: تو باشی here has the sense of a predicative subjunctive/optative meaning 'you will be' or 'may you be' rather than an imperative; classical Persian often uses باشی to indicate what will obtain for the addressee. بران is the fused form of بر + آن ('on/for that'), linking back to 'that portion.' در شهره‌ای is locative/benefactive: در ('in') + شهره‌ای (here 'renown, fame'), so the whole phrase says that, by that share, 'you will be in renown (well-known) in the city.'
segments.s352-g91: همت یار باشم is a poetic inversion: همت ('resolve/ambition') is placed first as the topic and یار باشم ('may I be a companion' / 'I will be an ally') is the predicate in the first person (باشم). The second half, همت کهترم, states humility: همت repeated as topic and کهترم ('I am the lesser' / 'my resolve is inferior') is an adjectival/predicative phrase with the first-person ending, meaning 'I am of lesser resolve.'
segments.s352-g92: که introduces the subordinate clause explaining or asserting the speaker's commitment: 'that' / 'for.' هرگز ('never') + ز ('from/of') + پیمان ('pledge, covenant') + تو ('your') gives 'never from your pledge' and نگذرم is the negative present/subjunctive 1st-person of گذشتن ('to pass/abandon'); together نگذرم = 'I will not pass from' i.e. 'I shall not break/abandon.' So the clause means 'that I will never break my pledge to you.'
segments.s352-g93: بدو گفت گشتاسپ is the inverted classical word order for 'Gushtasp said to him' (بدو = 'to him', گفت = past of 'say', گشتاسپ = the speaker's name). کاین سرد گشت is a reported clause introduced by کاین ('that/this'), with سرد ('stern/aloof') as predicate and گشت ('became') the verb: 'that this one became cold/aloof.'
segments.s352-g94: سخنها ('the words/speeches', plural) is the subject; ز اندازه ('from measure') is an idiomatic prepositional phrase meaning 'beyond measure' or 'past the proper bounds'; اندر گذشت is the verb phrase with گذشت ('passed') in the past tense: together 'the words passed beyond measure' i.e. 'the talk exceeded bounds.'
segments.s352-g95: تو کردی (you did) is simple past 'you did'; بدین is the prepositional phrase 'with/to this' (ب + این in classical orthography) and داوری ('judgment, decision') is its object. دست پیش is an idiomatic compound 'preemptive hand' meaning 'the initiative' or 'preemptive move.' Altogether: 'You have taken the initiative in this judgment.'
segments.s352-g96: کنون ('now') sets the time. بازگشتی is the verb 'you have turned back / you retract' (here second-person sense: you go back). ز گفتار خویش is 'from your own speech/word' (ز = from; گفتار = speech; خویش + ش is the possessive/reflective 'your own' / 'his own' in the classical split), so the clause means 'now you have withdrawn from your own words (you retract what you said).'
segments.s352-g73: همی + past verb (جست) is a classical Persian construction expressing a continuous or repeated past action: 'he kept leaping'. The locative phrase بر دشت جای نبرد (literally 'on the plain, place of battle') follows the verb and specifies where the leaping occurred; جای نبرد is a genitive apposition qualifying دشت.
segments.s352-g74: ز هامون sets the origin/source ('from the Hamun/plain'). The verbal phrase به ابر اندر آورد گرد is best read as 'brought/raised (آورد) گرد (here: dust) into/unto the clouds (به ابر اندر)': اندر + آورد = 'brought into', and گرد at the end is the noun 'dust' which is the thing brought up. Word order is poetic (object after the verb).
segments.s352-g75: چو + past (دید) introduces a temporal clause 'when Elias saw ...'. آن introduces the thing seen ('that'), and بر و یال اوی is a poetic co‑ordination; یال اوی is 'his mane', while بر here is part of the paired image ('بر و یال') describing the animal's neck/forepart and mane. اوی is the enclitic referring back to the possessor.
segments.s352-g76: چنان گردش = 'such/so (was) the turning/circle' (چنان qualifying گردش). چنگ و گوپال اوی form a coordinated image (two items being turned); چنگ is used image‑wise ('claw' or 'hoof' image) and گوپال here is a proper name or epithet attached by اوی ('his Gopāl'). The clause is descriptive/visual: 'such was the turning/whirl of X and Y.'
segments.s352-g77: سواری فرستاد is a simple past clause 'he sent a rider'; نزدیک اوی is a locative/adjunct 'near him' where اوی is the third‑person enclitic pronoun referring to the person already in view. Word order: object (سواری) + verb (فرستاد) + adjunct.
segments.s352-g78: که introduces a purpose/result clause here ('so that / in order that'). بفریبد is the present/subjunctive form (ب + present stem) meaning 'may/might deceive or charm'. ان رای تاریک اوی is the object phrase 'that dark counsel of his' (ان = 'that', رای = 'counsel/plan', تاریک = 'dark/obscure', اوی = possessive 'his').
segments.s352-g79: بیامد is past 'he came'; بدو is the indirect object 'to him' (بد + او), and گفت is the past 'said' introducing direct speech. کای is a vocative particle 'O' here addressing someone, and سرفراز is the epithet 'honoured/ proud one' used vocatively.
segments.s352-g80: ز قیصر here indicates relation to or because of the Caesar ('with/regarding the Caesar'). بدین گونه is the fixed phrase 'in this manner' (ب functioning as the prepositional/prefixal element + this/way), and سر کم فراز is a descriptive phrase: سر 'head' + کم فراز 'less raised' (i.e. 'bowed/low'); together: 'your head is thus low' or 'your head hangs low in this way before the Caesar.'
segments.s352-g41: اگر تاب داری is a protasis (conditional): اگر = 'if', تاب = 'strength/endurance', داری = 2nd-person present 'you have'. به + [جنگ]-ش + بگوی: به here marks direction/target ('to'), جنگش = جنگ + enclitic -ش 'his/him' meaning 'a fight with him' or 'his combat', and بگوی is an instructive/subjunctive form 'tell (so that)...' (in classical style used to mean 'tell/command'). The clause therefore means 'If you have the strength, tell him to fight.'
segments.s352-g42: و گرنه = 'and if not / otherwise' (گرنه = 'otherwise'). مبر is the negative imperative 'do not take/bring'. اندرین = 'in/onto this' (an older form of در + این), آب = 'water', روی = 'surface/face (of)'. Together: 'and if not, do not bring (him) onto/in these waters.'
segments.s352-g43: اگر ... نداری = 'if you do not have / if you are not in' — here جنگ او را نداری literally 'you have not war with him' = 'you are not at war with him / you do not wish to fight him'. تو is the addressee 'you' (vocative/emphatic) and پای here means 'a footing/settlement/pact' (classical use 'پای ساختن' = to set a basis or settle). The clause sets the condition and names the object (a pact) to be arranged.
segments.s352-g44: بسازیم is 1st-person plural 'we will make/construct' (future/optative sense in context). با او = 'with him'. یکی خوب رای = 'one good counsel/plan' — یکی (a single), خوب (good), رای (counsel/plan). Word order shows the verb up front followed by the prepositional phrase and the noun phrase (poetic inversion).
segments.s352-g45: به خوبی = 'with kindness/gently' (به + خوبی as an adverbial phrase). ز ره = 'from the way/road' (ز = 'from', ره = 'road'). بازگردانمش is the verb 'I will return/bring back him': بازگردان + م (1st-person) + ش (3rd-person object enclitic). The clause means 'I will turn him back from the road kindly.'
segments.s352-g46: سخن is the thing to be given: 'speech/words'. با هزینه = 'with expense/at cost' (indicating lavish spending). برافشانمش is a compound verb b-r-afshān- + م (I) + ش (him/it) meaning literally 'I will scatter/heap/give lavishly to him/it'; here it means 'I will lavish it (speech) upon him, even at cost.'
segments.s352-g47: بدو گفت گشتاسپ is inverted word order: بدو = 'to him' (indirect object), گفت = past 'said' (verb), گشتاسپ = the speaker's name (subject). کین جست و جوی: کین = 'vengeance/hatred' (object), جست = past 'sought' (he sought), و جوی forms the verbal noun/compound 'جست و جوی' = 'seeking/inquiry' — overall part of Gushtasp's speech: 'You sought vengeance / you seek vengeance...'.
segments.s352-g48: چرا باید و چیست = a two-part interrogative: چرا = 'why', باید = modal 'is it necessary / ought', و = 'and', چیست = 'what is' (چی + ست inverted to چیست). این گفت و گوی = 'this talk/conversation' (گفت + و + گوی forming the compound 'گفت‌وگو'). Together: 'Why is it necessary, and what is this conversation?'

sentences.s352-s49: Now speaking is of no use when there is war and the grapple of battle.
sentences.s352-s50: The messenger returned and came like the wind; he answered, remembering Elias.
sentences.s352-s33: From the side of the Caspian an iron pipe (trumpet) rose; a dust‑whirl rose up and turned toward the right of the sky.
sentences.s352-s34: The proud Caesar said to Gushtasp, 'Now bring the army forth from its concealment.'
sentences.s352-s35: He said this and drew his host out; he led the champions and warriors onto the plain.
sentences.s352-s36: He went about with his mace, Gausar, like a tall cypress rising from the mountain-side.
sentences.s352-s25: As long as I am on this earth in the world, I have no fear of the Khazar frontier.
sentences.s352-s26: But one should not, on the day of battle, call to mind (or mention) Mirīn and Ahrən.
sentences.s352-s27: for (or lest) they, in battle, out of enmity, may bring forth crookedness and demonic (Ahrimanic) evil.
sentences.s352-s28: If an army should come from the Khazar frontier, be my guardian with one son.
sentences.s352-s13: When the news reached Mīrīn and Ahrān about Elyās and the trap he had spread.
sentences.s352-s14: Mīrīn sent a message to the Caesar, saying that this dragon is not one to be trapped.
sentences.s352-s15: He is no wolf who, for lack of stratagem, would be made lifeless by being tainted with treacherous poison.
sentences.s352-s16: When Elyās in battle grew angry, he made the world-seeker's eyes run with blood.
sentences.s352-s29: By the victorious power of one god, when I come forth with an army from my place.
sentences.s352-s30: I am not Elias, nor do I have an army with him; nor such greatness, throne, and helmet.
sentences.s352-s31: I seize his belt, and from behind the saddle I lift him into the clouds; I strike him down to the ground.
sentences.s352-s32: On another day, when the sun rose, like a golden shield it appeared upon the water.
sentences.s352-s5: If Sāv and Bājest and costly treasure, and the hostages from that frontier, (are) to some chiefs.
sentences.s352-s6: Otherwise Ferokhzād, like a drunken elephant, will come and take your realm in his hand.
sentences.s352-s7: When Elyās read that letter, he dipped the tip of his pen into poisonous water.
sentences.s352-s8: He replied thus: 'You had little skill in Rome throughout.'
sentences.s352-s41: From this army you are now its horseman; you are its spring; you are its famed one.
sentences.s352-s42: Why have you leaned aside out from between the two ranks, and what have you that you thus bring your palm to your lips?
sentences.s352-s43: For Elyās is a lion on the day of battle; he enters (or is received into shelter) more lightly than dust.
sentences.s352-s44: If you want a gift for him, there is treasure; but later (in the evening), to obtain anything comes only with toil of the hand.
sentences.s352-s9: If I do not wish to go to Rome thus, may you be happy with that realm and land.
sentences.s352-s10: So you were distressed by a single horseman; when he was found near you — take heed!
sentences.s352-s11: Consider it so: he is a snare of Ahriman; and if (even) he be a mountain of iron, yet he is but one man.
sentences.s352-s12: Do not trouble him in this battle, for I shall not linger long in speech.
sentences.s352-s1: The Khazars were nearer to the Caesar, and because of them his days grew darker.
sentences.s352-s2: On the Khazar frontier the chief was Elias, who was the son of Jahandar Mehras.
sentences.s352-s3: The Caesar sent a letter to Elias; you said that he stained the tip of his pen with blood.
sentences.s352-s4: For the Khazars had endured many a sorrow; now a day of ease has come upon them.
sentences.s352-s17: Look now — see how this proud man turns toward the plain of battle.
sentences.s352-s18: Caesar was filled with sorrow at their words when he heard of that kind of their marketplace.
sentences.s352-s19: He said to Farrokhzad, 'A man rich in wealth — I will (go to) Rome like an ornament.'
sentences.s352-s20: Consider him so: Elias is the mighty Shirā-oozhān; when the steed threw (him), he was an iron‑bodied elephant.
sentences.s352-s45: Choose one portion from the world; by that portion you will be famed in the city.
sentences.s352-s46: May my resolve be your companion — my resolve is the lesser — for I will never break my pledge to you.
sentences.s352-s47: Gushtasp said to him: 'This one has grown cold/aloof.'
sentences.s352-s48: You have, by this judgment, taken the initiative — now you withdraw from your own words.
sentences.s352-s37: He kept leaping upon the plain, the place of battle; from the Hamun he lifted dust up to the clouds.
sentences.s352-s38: When Elias saw that neck and mane of his, such was the whirl of his claw and (his) Gopāl.
sentences.s352-s39: He sent a rider near him, so that he might deceive him with that dark counsel of his.
sentences.s352-s40: He came to him and said, 'O honoured one, before the Caesar your head is thus low.'
sentences.s352-s21: If you have the strength, tell him to fight; otherwise do not bring him onto these waters.
sentences.s352-s22: If you have no quarrel with him, we will establish for you a pact; we will make with him one good plan.
sentences.s352-s23: I will turn him back from the road gently; I will lavish speech on him at expense.
sentences.s352-s24: Gushtasp said to him: 'Why seek vengeance, and what is this talk?'.
